content:
5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Stop copy-pasting skills between Claude, Cursor, and Gemini. Declare them once, install them everywhere.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

agentry — pip for AI coding agentsOpen your editor’s hidden folders and take an honest look.
There’s a .claude/ directory with a code-reviewer skill in it. There's a .cursor/ directory with — wait, is that the same code-reviewer, copied over by hand three weeks ago? And a .gemini/ folder where you meant to add it but never got around to it. One of the three has a fix the others don't. You're not sure which.
This is the daily reality of working with more than one AI coding agent. Every tool invented its own folder, its own file format, its own conventions — and left you, the human, as the integration layer. You are the one shuttling files between .claude/, .cursor/, .gemini/, and .opencode/. You are the one who notices, eventually, that they've drifted out of sync.
We’ve seen this movie before. It’s dependency hell — the exact problem pip, yarn, and uv solved for software packages decades ago. Nobody hand-copies a library into five projects anymore. So why are we doing it with AI components?
So we built the missing tool: agentry (command: agy), a dependency manager for AI coding agents.

The problem isn’t your agents. It’s the gap between them.
The AI ecosystem is expanding faster than it’s standardizing. Each agent is genuinely useful. But each one also reads its components from a different place, in a different shape:

• Claude Code reads .claude/skills/, .claude/agents/, .claude/commands/, .claude/settings.json, and .mcp.json.

• Cursor reads .cursor/rules/ and .cursor/mcp.json.

• OpenCode reads .opencode/… and opencode.json.

• …and so on for Gemini CLI, Codex, Windsurf, and Kimi.
A skill is a skill, a hook is a hook, an MCP server is an MCP server — the same idea, just expressed in seven incompatible dialects. The moment you use more than one agent, you inherit every failure software packaging already eliminated: no versioning, no single source of truth, duplicated effort across projects, drift between machines, and no safe way to uninstall.
You’re not managing AI components. You’re babysitting copies of them.

The insight: these are just packages
Here’s the reframe that makes everything click. Skills, agents, commands, tools, hooks, and MCP servers are packages. They have names. They have versions. They come from somewhere. They have dependencies. They get installed into a target.
That is precisely the shape of a software dependency. And we already know how to manage software dependencies: you declare what you want in one file, pin exact versions in a lockfile, and let a tool install everything reproducibly.
agentry brings that model to AI components. You declare your AI dependencies once; one command installs them into every agent you target — each in its own native layout.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Declare once on the left. agy sync. Installed everywhere on the right. That's the whole idea.

How it works: three files, one command
agentry treats AI components like packages, with the same three-part model that every good package manager uses:

• .agentry.yml — a single, version-controlled file declaring your sources and components. This is your intent. You hand-edit it (or let the CLI edit it for you), and you commit it.

• .agentry.lock — the exact resolved commit SHAs for deterministic, reproducible installs. Same lock, same install, on every machine and in CI.

• .agentry/ — a local store of downloaded git clones and copies, git-ignored exactly like node_modules.
One agy sync reconciles everything to match your declaration. File and directory components (skills, agents, commands, tools) install via symlink, so they live-update straight from the store. Config-based components (hooks, MCP servers) install via reversible config merges — agentry injects exactly the keys it owns and can strip exactly those keys back out, never touching anything you added by hand.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

And one detail worth saying plainly, because it’s a design principle, not an afterthought:
agentry is a dependency manager, not an agent or a runtime. It installs the components your agents read, then gets out of the way — nothing of it runs while your agents do.

Quickstart in six lines
Here’s the entire flow, start to finish:
agy init --target claude --target opencode # create .agentry.yml + .gitignore
agy source add team-skills https://github.com/org/team-skills --ref main
agy list # see what's available
agy add team-skills/skill/code-reviewer # enable + install a skill
agy add team-skills/mcp/github # merge an MCP server into .mcp.json
agy sync # reconcile to match config + lockagy init writes your .agentry.yml and adds .agentry/ to .gitignore. agy source add registers a repo to pull components from. agy add enables a component and installs it. agy sync makes the disk match your declared, pinned intent — and it's idempotent, so running it twice does nothing the second time.
Commit .agentry.yml and .agentry.lock. Now any teammate who clones the repo runs one command — agy sync — and gets the exact same set of skills, agents, and MCP servers, installed into whichever agents they use. No copy-paste. No drift. No "which folder has the fix?"

Install it in one line
macOS / Linux:
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenTechIL/agentry/main/install.sh | shWindows (PowerShell):
irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenTechIL/agentry/main/install.ps1 | iexNo Python required — the installer grabs the right standalone binary for your OS and architecture, verifies its checksum, and drops agy on your PATH. Verify with agy version.
Prefer Python tooling? uvx --from git+https://github.com/OpenTechIL/agentry agy <command> runs it straight from git with no install at all.

There’s a starter catalog, too
Don’t want to hunt for repos? agentry ships a starter catalog — a name-to-source mapping so you can install components by name without knowing the URL or the flags:
agy catalog add default https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenTechIL/agentry/main/registry/repositories.json
agy add arckit # the whole repo — every component it provides
agy add arckit --type skill # just the skillsFour curated repos ship in it today — arckit, ui-ux-pro-max, graphify, and superpowers — and it's an open registry: you can contribute your own repo with a single PR.

Stop being the integration layer
You didn’t get into this work to hand-copy markdown files between hidden folders and play spot-the-difference when one of them breaks. Declare your AI components once. Let agy sync install them everywhere. Commit the result so your whole team is reproducibly in sync.

• ⭐ Star and explore the repo: github.com/OpenTechIL/agentry

• 📦 Install: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenTechIL/agentry/main/install.sh | sh
Write once, deploy to any agent.
